title: Command Injection
description:
A utility endpoint pings a user-supplied hostname using a shell command built via string concatenation. An attacker can append shell metacharacters (`;`, `&&`, `|`) to run arbitrary commands on the server.

Fix `ping_host()` to pass arguments as a list and avoid shell=True.
hint: Use `subprocess.run(["ping", "-c", "1", hostname], shell=False)` and validate that hostname contains only safe characters.
explanation: Passing user input directly to a shell command allows attackers to chain additional commands using `;`, `&&`, or `|`. Using `subprocess.run` with a list (not a string) and `shell=False` prevents the shell from interpreting metacharacters.

attack_flow.label: Result
attack_flow.value: uid=0(root) gid=0(root) groups=0(root)  ← OS command executed

why_blocked: By passing arguments as a list with `shell=False`, Python's subprocess hands each argument directly to the OS — no shell interpreter is involved. Metacharacters like `;` are treated as literal data, not shell syntax, so the injected `id` command never runs.
